Details: Chlorine can be used for free cyanide removal; however, the end products are dependent of pH levels. At pH below 8.5, cyanogen chloride (CNCl), a highly toxic and volatile compound, is formed [1364]. With pH increased to above 8.5, cyanogen chloride can be converted to cyanate (CNO-), which is several hundred times less toxic than cyanides [1364]. In addition, the conversion time to cyanate decreases significantly with increased pH [1364]. Therefore, pH needs to be adjusted to above 8.5 to avoid formation of cyanogen chloride, or typically above 11.5 to obtain rapid oxidation to cyanate. To oxidize cyanate, pH needs to be reduced to below 9.5 [1365]. Chlorine is commonly used for cyanide removal in wastewaters; however, limited data are found for drinking water treatment. A bench-scale lab study showed that cyanide was reduced from 100, 250 and 500 μg/L to below 5 μg/L with a free chlorine residual of 1 mg/L [1362]. However, the end products were not identified. A bench-scale study of shipboard wastewater found 58 to 97 percent cyanide oxidation from a starting concentration of 3.9 mg/L, depending on the chlorine dose applied [2461].
